vulnerability.title: Disable GSSAPI Authentication
vulnerability.description: Unless needed, SSH should not permit extraneous or unnecessary authentication mechanisms like GSSAPI. The default SSH configuration disallows authentications based on GSSAPI. The appropriate configuration is used if no value is set for GSSAPIAuthentication. To explicitly disable GSSAPI authentication, add or correct the following line in /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf: GSSAPIAuthentication no
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core; then mkdir -p /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d touch /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/01-complianceascode-reinforce-os-defaults.conf chmod 0600 /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/01-complianceascode-reinforce-os-defaults.conf LC_ALL=C sed -i "/^\s*GSSAPIAuthentication\s\+/Id" "/etc/ssh/sshd_config" LC_ALL=C sed -i "/^\s*GSSAPIAuthentication\s\+/Id" "/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d"/*.conf if [ -e "/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/01-complianceascode-reinforce-os-defaults.conf" ] ; then LC_ALL=C sed -i "/^\s*GSSAPIAuthentication\s\+/Id" "/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/01-complianceascode-reinforce-os-defaults.conf" else touch "/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/01-complianceascode-reinforce-os-defaults.conf" fi # make sure file has newline at the end sed -i -e '$a\' "/etc/ssh/

triage_decision.reason: Disabling GSSAPIAuthentication is a standard SSH hardening step that does not risk lockout or system breakage; it simply removes an optional authentication method.
triage_decision.estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Potential change to SSH authentication methods

llm_verdict.message:
**Why the remediation isn’t succeeding** When the script tried to read or write the file **`/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00‑complianceascode-hardening.conf`** the target system answered: ``` You are required to change your password immediately (password expired). WARNING: Your password has expired. Password change required but no TTY available. ``` That means the root account’s password is expired and the operating system is forcibly blocking any file‑modification or command‑execution that would touch system files until an interactive password change is completed. Because we (the automation session) can’t supply a new password interactively, every attempt to `read_file` or `write_file` fails with that error. --- ### How to get the system into a state where the fix can be applied | Step | What to do | Command‑line example | |------|------------|----------------------| | **1** | **Reset the root password** (or set a non‑expiring password). | ```passwd root``` | | **2** | After the password has been set, verify that you can now read/write files under `/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d`. | ```cat /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d``` | | **3** | **Create / edit** the hardening include file to disable GSSAPI authentication. | ```bash`
``` echo "GSSAPIAuthentication no" > /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf
chmod 0600 /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf ``` | | **4** | **Reload or restart SSH** so the change takes effect (this may momentarily drop existing SSH sessions). | ```systemctl reload sshd``` or ```systemctl restart sshd``` | | **5** | Re‑run the OpenSCAP scan (or the specific check) to confirm that the **`sshd_disable_gssapi_auth`** finding is resolved. | “run your scan tool again” | *Tip:* If the `/etc/ssh/sshd_config` file already contains an `Include /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/*.conf` line, the new file will be picked up automatically. If not, add/verify that line before the reload—otherwise the change wouldn’t have any effect at all. --- ### Summary - The password‑expired error is why the remediation tool cannot write the required line. - Resetting the root password (or making it never expire) will allow the script to create or update `/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf`. - After that, reload SSH to apply the change and re‑run the scan to confirm compliance. Let me know if you need help resetting the password or want to verify that the file was created correctly after you have root access!

reasoning_messages.content:
I’m sorry, but I’m unable to make the required changes at this time. When trying to read or write files in `/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d`, the system returns:

```
You are required to change your password immediately (password expired).
WARNING: Your password has expired.
Password change required but no TTY available.
```

This indicates that the root account’s password has expired, and the system is forcing an interactive password change before allowing any file modifications. Because the execution environment is non‑interactive, I cannot complete the file creation or edit that would set `GSSAPIAuthentication no`.

Once the root password has been reset and a non‑expiring password is in place, or when you can run these commands with proper authentication, you can apply the remediation by creating or updating `/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf` with the line:

```
GSSAPIAuthentication no
```

After that, run `systemctl reload sshd` to apply the change (this will not break existing SSH sessions).

Then we attempted to read file with read_file; the output indicates some password expiration problem: "You are required to change your password immediately (password expired). ...". So the underlying environment has password expiration issues.

The assistant attempted to perform read_file, write_file but the system responded that root password expired. There's no tool to handle that.

We need to see if we can fix this.

The user: “You are the Remedy agent remediating ONE OpenSCAP finding on Rocky Linux 10. ... ” And eventually the assistant returned a final. But the last assistant said "Sorry, I'm unable to make required changes due to password expiration." So the question: The system has expiration; we cannot proceed. But we could attempt to circumvent or at least note the error.

But is there a possible solution? We could try to use `ssh-agent` or similar. But the system constraints: use only run_cmd, read_file, write_file. There's no ability to change root password in this environment. It's likely that we cannot write the file due to password being expired. There's no support for interactive command. But the environment might not have the ability for sudo or root modifications. We can't change root password.

We could suggest something: if we can't modify due to password expiration, we could try to use `patch` or `sed` and get error? But we still would need permission to write. Since we can't modify root file due to password being expired, we can't proceed.

Maybe we should respond with: we cannot proceed because root password is expired, we need to either reset password or supply credential. Or mention that the session is not allowed to modify /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d due to root password expired.

But we need to produce a final answer. We should give an apology and explanation that the environment doesn't allow modifications because root password has expired. The correct remediation steps are to set root password or run commands interactively.

Thus final.
reasoning_messages.content:
I’m sorry, but I’m not able to apply the change at this time.  
When the script attempts to read or write files under **/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d**, the system reports:

```
You are required to change your password immediately (password expired).
WARNING: Your password has expired.
Password change required but no TTY available.
```

This indicates that the root account’s password is expired and the system is forcing an interactive password change before allowing any file modifications. Because the current session cannot provide interactive input, the file operations fail and the remediation cannot be performed.

### What you can do next

1. **Reset the root password** (or create a non‑expiring password) so that the system no longer blocks file changes.  
   Example (run on the target host as an administrator with a working root password):

   ```bash
   passwd root
   ```

   Follow the prompts to set a new password.

2. **After the password reset** run the same remediation steps again:

   - Create or edit  
     `/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf`  
     and add the line:

     ```
     GSSAPIAuthentication no
     ```

   - (Optional) Reload SSH to apply the change:

     ```bash
     systemctl reload sshd
     ```

3. **Re‑run the OpenSCAP scan** to verify that `sshd_disable_gssapi_auth` is now satisfied.

If you have any trouble resetting the password or applying the change after that, let me know—I’ll be glad to help walk through the steps.

reasoning_messages.reasoning: The assistant attempts to apply plan but fails due to root password expired. The solution is correct. Probably no action needed.

When attempting to read or write files in `/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d`, the system returned:

```

WARNING: Your password has expired.
Password change required but no TTY available.
```

This means the root account’s password has expired and the system requires an interactive password change before it will allow any file modifications. Because the session cannot provide that interactive input, the file operations failed.

**Next steps to remediate**

1. **Reset the root password** (or set a non‑expiring password) so that file changes are permitted.  
   Example (run on the host with existing root access):

   ```bash
   passwd root
   ```

   Provide a new password when prompted.

2. **After the password reset** try the remediation again:
   - Create or edit `/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf` and add the line

     ```plaintext
     GSSAPIAuthentication no
     ```

   - Optionally reload SSH to apply the setting:

     ```bash
     systemctl reload sshd
     ```

3. **Re‑run the OpenSCAP scan** to confirm that the `sshd_disable_gssapi_auth` finding is fixed.

If you hit any issues while resetting the password or applying the change afterward, let me know and I can guide you further.

llm_verdict.message:
I’m sorry, but I wasn’t able to apply the change because the session’s root account is currently blocked by an expired password.  
When I tried to read `/etc/ssh/sshd_config` or create the drop‑in file, the system returned:

```
You are required to change your password immediately (password expired).
WARNING: Your password has expired.
Password change required but no TTY available.
```

Hence all subsequent `run_cmd` calls failed.

**What you can do next**

1. **Reset the root password**  
   From a console or TTY, run `passwd` and set a new password for root (or enable another privileged user).

2. **Re‑run the remediation**  
   Once root access is restored, follow the plan I outlined earlier:
   * Make sure `/etc/ssh/sshd_config` has `Include /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/*.conf` (add it if missing).
   * Create or update `/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf` with the line `GSSAPIAuthentication no` and chmod it to 0600.
   * Optionally run `systemctl reload sshd` to apply the changes immediately.

Let me know when root is available, and I’ll re‑apply the fix for you.

reasoning_messages.content:
I was unable to proceed because the environment is refusing to run any commands that require root privileges. When I attempted to read `/etc/ssh/sshd_config`, the system returned

```
You are required to change your password immediately (password expired).
WARNING: Your password has expired.
Password change required but no TTY available.
```

and every subsequent command that tries to touch files or create the drop‑in directory also fails with the same message.  

Since the remediation requires modification of `/etc/ssh/sshd_config` and the creation of a file in `/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d`, it cannot be applied without a functioning root shell. Once root authentication is restored (e.g., by resetting the expired password via `passwd` or by using an account that has the necessary permissions), the plan I outlined can be executed immediately.

reasoning_messages.content:
I’m sorry I wasn’t able to apply the change.  
The commands that touch `/etc/ssh/sshd_config` and create the drop‑in file in `/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d` need root access. The current environment is refusing the calls because the root account’s password is expired and cannot be changed interactively (no TTY is available).  

Once the root password has been reset (e.g., by running `passwd` on a console or by enabling a privileged user that can execute `sudo -i`), you can run the plan I outlined:

1. Ensure `/etc/ssh/sshd_config` contains an `Include /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/*.conf` line.
2. Create or update `/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf` with `GSSAPIAuthentication no` and permission 0600.
3. Optionally reload sshd with `systemctl reload sshd`.

If you have any questions about the steps once root is available, feel free to let me know.

reasoning_messages.content:
I’m sorry, but I couldn’t carry out the remediation steps because the environment blocks root‑level commands at the moment.  
When I attempted to read `/etc/ssh/sshd_config` or create the drop‑in directory, the system returned a “password expired” message:

```
You are required to change your password immediately (password expired).
WARNING: Your password has expired.
Password change required but no TTY available.
```

Consequently all subsequent `run_cmd` attempts (e.g., `mkdir`, `touch`, `sed`, etc.) also failed with the same error.

### What needs to be done next

1. **Reset the root password**  
   Run `passwd` from a console/TTY to change the expired root password, or enable a privileged user that already has the necessary permissions.

2. **Once root access is restored, re‑apply the plan**  
   * Verify that `/etc/ssh/sshd_config` contains an `Include /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/*.conf` line, adding it if missing.  
   * Ensure the drop‑in file `/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf` exists, contains `GSSAPIAuthentication no`, and is chmod‑0600.  
   * Optionally run `systemctl reload sshd` so the change takes effect immediately.

Please let me know when root access is available, and I’ll re‑apply the remediation steps for you.

**What you can do next**

previous_review_verdicts.feedback: The plan correctly implements the disabled GSSAPI authentication directive via an include file and preserves file hygiene. Minor improvements can be made for completeness.
concerns: If the system's sshd_config does not include the .d directory, the change will have no effect; the plan assumes such inclusion. The plan does not trigger a reload of sshd, so changes may not take effect until the daemon is restarted or reloaded.
suggested_improvements: Verify that /etc/ssh/sshd_config contains an 'Include /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/*.conf' directive or add it.
After writing the file, consider instructing the operator to run 'systemctl reload sshd' or 'systemctl restart sshd' to apply changes, noting that this might temporarily disrupt connections.

previous_review_verdicts.feedback: The plan correctly adds / ensures the GSSAPIAuthentication no directive via a drop‑in file and verifies that the Include line is present, so it will disable GSSAPI authentication once the SSH daemon is reloaded.
concerns: The drop‑in file’s permissions are not explicitly set to 0600 in the provided commands, which is recommended for SSH configuration files.
The plan does not trigger a systemctl reload or restart of sshd, so changes will not take effect until a manual reload is performed.

If the drop‑in file already exists with multiple GSSAPIAuthentication entries the sed operation will remove all but the last, which is acceptable but could risk accidental removal of an intentional “no” set earlier.
The plan does not ensure a final newline at the end of the drop‑in file, which is a minor but common SSHfile requirement.
suggested_improvements: Add a command to set the file mode to 0600 after writing: `run_cmd 'chmod 0600 /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf'`.
Include an optional step to reload sshd, e.g., `run_cmd 'systemctl reload sshd'`, or instruct operators to do so after applying changes.
Use a sed or oprate that ensures no duplicate GSSAPIAuthentication directives are added; maybe check for existing 'no' before appending.
Append a final newline to the drop‑in file if it does not already have one, e.g., `run_cmd 'printf "\n" >> /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf'`.
